coredata.dc:title: Facile marriage of Gd3+ to polymer-coated carbon nanodots with enhanced biocompatibility for targeted MR/fluorescence imaging of glioma
coredata.dc:creator: Du, Yilin
Qian, Min
Li, Chengyi
Jiang, Huiling
Yang, Yafeng
Huang, Rongqin
coredata.dc:description: Overcoming blood-brain barrier (BBB) for precise glioma diagnosis remains an urgent challenge due to its peculiar location in central nervous system (CNS). Herein, polymer-coated carbon nanodots with high hydrophilicity were facilely married with Gd-DTPA to construct a dual-modal imaging system (NCDDG). This system was demonstrated with obviously decreased toxicity and enhanced magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) ability compared to traditional Gd-DTPA. Meanwhile, NCDDG reserved the bright fluorescence of biocompatible carbon nanodots with increased spatial resolution. Attributed to small size and hydrophilic polymer coating, NCDDG was capable of overcoming the BBB and permeating leaky microvascular walls into surrounding glioma tissues via prolonged in vivo circulation and enhanced retention effect. As a result, dual1-modal targeted MR/fluorescence imaging of glioma was synergistically achieved with high sensitivity and resolution. This work promised a potential contrast agent for sensitive clinical diagnosis of glioma.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nanoprobe
Glioma
Carbon nanodots
Fluorescence imaging
MRI

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of polymer-coated nitrogen-doped carbon dots (NCD)

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: a, b) HRTEM images of NCDDG and the corresponding FFT pattern (c). The inset in (a) is inverse Fourier transform of the HRTEM image. d) EDX image of NCDDG from (a). e) FTIR spectra of NCD, NCDD and NCDDG. f) XPS survey scan spectra of NCDDG. g) Zeta potential of NCD, NCDD and NCDDG (n = 3). h) DLS data of NCDDG and NCD at different time points.

a) Two-dimensional fluorescence spectrum of NCDDG. The inserted images are optical images of NCDDG aqueous solution excited by a 365 nm-UV lamp (left) and daylight (right), respectively. b) T1W-MR signal change of NCDDG and commercial Gd-DTPA at different Gd3+ concentration. c) 1/T1 of NCDDG and commercial Gd-DTPA as a function of Gd3+ concentration.

a) CLSM images of U87 cells cultured with NCDDG of 300 μg/mL for different time periods (0.5 h, 1 h, 2 h and 4 h). The left line are bright field images, the middle line are fluorescent images under 405 nm excitation and the right line are merged images. b) CLSM images of U87 cells cultured with NCDDG of different concentration (50 μg/mL, 150 μg/mL, 300 μg/mL, 600 μg/mL) for 2 h. Bar = 100 μm.
A comparison of U87 cell viability between NCDDG and commercial Gd-DTPA with Gd3+ at different concentrations under 24 h incubation. Significance: ***, p < 0.001.

a) T1W-MR images of a representative nude mouse under different time periods (0 h, 0.5 h and 1 h) preinjected with NCDDG. b) The T1-MR signal to background percentage of margin and central part in tumor over time (0 h, 0.5 h and 1 h). The signal values were obtained from the calculation of medical professional image processing software MRIcro to circled area where red, yellow and blue circled area represent central part, margin of the glioma and background, respectively. c) Fluorescence images of a representative nude mouse at different time periods (0 h, 0.5 h, 1 h and 1.5 h). d) Fluorescence images of main organs (from left to right: brain, heart, liver, spleen, lung and kidney). e) Fluorescence images of two cuts of brain from (d). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
a, b) Illustration of the facile method to prepare NCD married with clinical Gd-DTPA. c) Fate of NCDDG administrated intravenously in vivo and its major biological effects.

head.ce:title: Facile marriage of Gd3+ to polymer-coated carbon nanodots with enhanced biocompatibility for targeted MR/fluorescence imaging of glioma

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Pharmaceutics, School of Pharmacy, Key Laboratory of Smart Drug Delivery, Ministry of Education, Fudan University, Shanghai 201203, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Smart Drug Delivery

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Illustration of the facile to prepare NCDDG (A) and NCDDG (B) and Fate of NCDDG (G) administrated intravenously in vivo and its biological effects.

Overcoming blood-brain barrier (BBB) for precise glioma diagnosis remains an urgent challenge due to its peculiar location in central nervous system (CNS). Herein, polymer-coated carbon nanodots with high hydrophilicity were facilely married with Gd-DTPA to construct a dual-modal imaging system (NCDDG). This system was demonstrated with obviously decreased toxicity and enhanced magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) ability compared to traditional Gd-DTPA. Meanwhile, NCDDG reserved the bright fluorescence of biocompatible carbon nanodots with increased spatial resolution. Attributed to small size and hydrophilic polymer coating, NCDDG was capable of overcoming the BBB and permeating leaky microvascular walls into surrounding glioma tissues via prolonged in vivo circulation and enhanced retention effect. As a result, dual1-modal targeted MR/fluorescence imaging of glioma was synergistically achieved with high sensitivity and resolution. This work promised a potential contrast agent for sensitive clinical diagnosis of glioma.

ce:section.ce:para: Glioma is one of the most malignant tumors in CNS due to its high mortality rate. Throughout the treatment and monitoring, imaging techniques, including MRI, computed tomography (CT), single photon emission computed tomography (SPECT), positron emission tomography (PET), ultrasonic imaging (USI) and X-ray imaging, play a critical role in the glioma diagnosis, guidance to the surgery and indication of the therapeutic outcome (Chow and Paramesran, 2016). Nevertheless, owing to the special location of glioma in CNS, single imaging provides unabundant information about the lesion site, which therefore might guide many false positives (Lin et al., 2016). Accordingly, multimodal imaging is now emerging as a widely accepted paradigm because it can not only enrich the lesion information but endow higher sensitivity and precision as well.

Among various imaging techniques, traditional MRI receives great favor due to its non-invasion, high spatial resolution and excellent soft tissue contrast and is considered to be more suitable for glioma imaging (Sosnovik and Weissleder, 2005). However, it often suffers from lower sensitivity for small lesions in complex physiological environment (Zhou et al., 2017). Although diverse contrast agents have been used to enhance the sensitivity, shortcomings such as non-specificity, short residence time, lower proton relaxation efficiency and potential toxicity are still existing for both small molecule and some nanoscaled contrast agents (Ni et al., 2017). Especially, the structure associated with the properties of these contrast agents must be elaborated to meet the demands of overcoming the BBB and glioma-targeted accumulation. Recently, fluorescence imaging has been regarded as a burgeoning tumor-imaging technique because of its high sensitivity, real-time monitoring, less harshness on the equipment and quantitative analysis capabilities (Key and Leary, 2014; Pysz et al., 2010). Meanwhile, using carbon nanodots as probes enhanced the practicality of tumor fluorescence imaging, because they abandon the unstable fluorescence emission of organic dyes/fluorescent proteins and the high cytotoxicity of semiconductor nanocrystals (Hahn et al., 2011; Le Trequesser et al., 2013; Luo et al., 2011; Padmanabhan et al., 2016; Qu et al., 2012; Yu et al., 2014). Especially, polymer-coated nitrogen-doped carbon nanodots (NCD) with subtle size, high water dispersity, and good biocompatibility have proved to be able to penetrate the BBB and accumulate into the tumor via enhanced permeability and retention (EPR) effect, which further offers the great potential for fluorescence imaging of glioma (Wang et al., 2015). However, just as stated above, single fluorescence imaging suffered from inadequate spatial resolution, which has to be combined with other techniques to achieve the consolidated glioma imaging.

In order to achieve precise diagnosis of glioma, the efficiency of overcoming BBB should be taken into consideration as well. Currently available strategies about glioma targeting mainly contain (1) penetrating through BBB assisted by some mediators such as BBB-related receptors or transporters, (2) temporarily opening the BBB through several physical and pharmacological methods, (3) directly targeting diseased cells in brain and (4) some dual targeting delivery from BBB to diseased cells (Gao, 2016, 2017). In this work, a highly water-soluble polymer-coated nitrogen-doped carbon nanodots were facilely married with widely used clinical Gd-DTPA contrast agent via covalent conjugation to construct a targeted MR/fluorescence imaging system (NCDDG). This system was not only able to decrease the toxicity and enhance the contrast ability of traditional Gd-DTPA via the highly hydrophilic polymer coating, but also increased the spatial resolution of carbon nanodots-based fluorescence imaging via MRI contrast. More importantly, although BBB permeability in normal brain tissues is lower than other biological barriers, it is increased in glioma patients owning to the destruction of the integrate BBB structure. Hence the prepared nanoprobe (NCDDG) might transport across the BBB based on the small hydrated particle size (30 nm) and passively target glioma via EPR effect (Ruan et al., 2014). As a result, dual-modal targeted MR/fluorescence imaging of glioma was synergistically achieved with high sensitivity and resolution.

Diethylenetriaminepentaacetic acid (DTPA), pyridine, acetic anhydride, N-methyl pyrrolidone (NMP), KH2PO4 and anhydrous ether were all purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd (China). Ultra-dry gadolinium chloride was purchased from China Shanghai Sigma Aldrich Trading Co., Ltd. As for cell culture, some reagents including Hank's solution, fetal bovine serum (FBS), phosphate buffer saline (PBS), penicillin and streptomycin were purchased from Hyclone, USA. The others including Dulbecco’s modified Eagle’s medium (DMEM), 0.25% trypsin and glutamine were purchased from GIBCO, USA. And clinically used Gd-DTPA was kindly provided by Nantong University Affiliated Hospital, China.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of polymer-coated nitrogen-doped carbon dots (NCD)
ce:section.ce:para: NCD was prepared via a modified method in our lab (Wang et al., 2015). First, 50 mL of NMP was added in an 80-mL Teflon-lined autoclave and hydrothermally reacted in the oven at 200 °C for about 12 h. Then the initial product was further purified by a vacuum rotary evaporation instrument and 3-day dialysis against water to remove the extra solvent and other impurities. At last, the final product NCD was dispersed in water or freeze-dried to powder for further use.

First of all, 7.8 g of DTPA, 8 mL of acetic anhydride and 10 mL of pyridine were put into a three-necked flask, stirred under reflux at 65 ± 4 °C for 24 h and then cooled to room temperature. After being washed with ether and acetic anhydride for several times, the product was placed and dried in a vacuum suction oven at about 80 °C for 12 h. Finally, cDTPAa was obtained and sealed in a desiccator.

Briefly, 2 mL (5 mg/mL) suspension of NCD in PBS (pH = 7.4) was prepared and then 0.01714 mol cDTPAa and 0.021 mol EDC were slowly added. After stirring at room temperature for 3 h, 0.034 mol GdCl3 was added. Finally, NCDDG was required by dialysis against water for two days to remove unreacted substance. The final product NCDDG was dispersed in water or freeze-dried to powder for further use.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were obtained from JEM-2010 instruments with an acceleration voltage of 200 kV. The solid NCDDG was re-dispersed in water evenly and then dried on an ultrathin carbon-coated copper grid. Zeta potential and particle size of NCDDG in solution were measured by a Malvern NanoZS zeta potential/particle sizer (Malvern Instruments Ltd., UK). Fourier Transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) spectroscopy using the KBr-pellet method was carried out on a Nicolet-360 FTIR spectrometer in the range of 400–4 000 cm−1. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) experiments were carried out on a RBD-upgraded PHI-5000C ESCA system (Perkin Elmer) with Al Kα radiation (hν = 1486.6 eV) as the X-ray source for excitation. Fluorescence excitation and emission spectra were measured on a Cary Eclipse fluorescence spectrophotometer (Varian, USA). Optical photographs were taken using a common Canon camera. In vitro MRI studies were conducted by a Siemens Trio TIM 3 T MRI scanner (Germany Siemens). Fluorescence lifetime results were tested through ISS ChronosDFD (USA). Confocal images were obtained using a Leica TCs SP5 microscope using a Leica application suite.

U87 cells were cultured in DMEM (10% FBS, 1% glutamine, 1% penicillin and 1% streptomycin) in a 37 °C and 5% CO2 incubator. After the cells adhering to the bottom of well, the whole medium was aspirated away under negative pressure, washed with PBS and then digested with 0.25% trypsin. The passage frequency was kept for every two or three days.

A dish of U87 cells was trypsinized and centrifuged to obtain the cell precipitation which was then diluted with media to 1 × 105/mL. Cell suspension (100 μL) was added in needy wells of 96-well plates at a density of 1 × 104 cells per well. Then the plate was incubated at 37 °C for 24 h. In the experiment, the medium was replaced by NCDDG solution at required concentration. After being incubated for a desired period of time, each well was washed with PBS and fixed with 4% paraformaldehyde. Confocal images were obtained with a Leica TCs SP5 microscope using a Leica application suite. Repetitive studies were carried out and 3 independent fields were observed in each study.

Nude male mice of about 20–22 g were provided by Shanghai Slac Laboratory Animal CO. LTD., and maintained under standard housing conditions. A stereotactic fixation device was used to slowly implant about 5 × 105 U87 cells into the right caudatoputamen. At the 18th day after implantation, the glioma-bearing mice were fasted one day to minimize food auto-fluorescence before the experiment. Then the mice were intravenously administered with the NCDDG solution at a dose of 50 mg/kg and fluorescence images ware taken with an IVIS Spectrum in-vivo imaging system (Caliper, USA) at various time points post-injection. Then the mice were killed by decapitation and main organs were removed and imaged. Finally, the glioma was dissected and imaged again. All animal experiments should comply with the ARRIVE guidelines and should be carried out in accordance with the National Institutes of Health guide for the care and use of Laboratory animals (NIH Publications No. 8023, revised 1978).

The animal preparation process in MRI experiment was the same as fluorescence imaging. The mice were administrated with NCDDG solution at a dose of 50 mg/kg. After the equipment was ready, the mice after anesthesia (3% isoflurane flow) were put in a 7.0 T micro-MRI scanner (Bruker Bio Spec, Germany) to be collected T1W-MR images within 60 min. All animal experiments should comply with the ARRIVE guidelines and should be carried out in accordance with the National Institutes of Health guide for the care and use of Laboratory animals (NIH Publications No. 8023, revised 1978).

According to Scheme 1 a, cDTPAa was firstly synthesized according to Hnatowich method (Hnatowich et al., 1982). Meanwhile, the hydrophilic NCD ample with functional groups such as COOH and NH2 which allowed further conjugation with cDTPAa were prepared by a simple one-step direct solvothermal reaction. The resulted NCDDG was obtained by the connection of cDTPAa and NCD through the carbodiimide method first and subsequently the strong chelation of DTPA with Gd3+ (Scheme 1b). As illustrated in Scheme 1c, well-dispersed NCDDG solution administrated intravenously followed the circulatory system into microvessels around tumor tissues and permeated sparse tight connection between BBB endothelial cells. Though the vasculature pore cutoff size in glioma is only 7–100 nm which is much lower than other peripheral tumors, NCDDG with the size around 30 nm still could target and accumulate in glioma via EPR effect (Ruan et al., 2014). Then assembled NCDDG with larger size contributed to its retention surrounding the glioma while dispersive probes were able to penetrate deeper to the tumor. Finally, due to the fluorescent and MR properties of NCDDG, dual-modal bioimaging could be realized in glioma diagnosis.

The as-prepared NCDDG observed from TEM images owned good dispersity, uniform particle size of about 5 nm (Fig. 1 a). However, the hydration radius of freshly prepared NCDDG was 31 nm but turned to 104 nm after 1 h compared to the relatively stable hydration radius of NCD in the same condition (Fig. 1h). This unique property that NCDDG tended to gather together and form larger particles with time might be explained by the decrease of hydrophilic amino groups on the surface of NCD and could facilitate the retention within the tumor tissue. Furthermore, this nanoscaled probe possessed crystalline core with d-spacing values of 0.22 nm regarded as (100) planes of graphitic carbon (Hola et al., 2017) and polymer-like shell (Fig. 1b and c). Meanwhile EDX from TEM images offered a compelling evidence to the successful marriage of Gd3+ (Fig. 1d). Additionally, extra data were obtained herein to demonstrate the successful preparation and diverse properties of NCDDG step by step, At the very beginning, through FTIR spectra, the derivation of cDTPAa was proved by a characteristic cleavage peak of vC=O at 1820 cm−1 and 1780 cm−1 which belonged to cDTPAa and the formation of amide bond could be affirmed where the absorption peaks of vC=O from NCD, NCDD and NCDDG migrated slightly from 1680 cm−1 to 1655 cm−1 and the absorption peaks of vC-N was strengthened for the successful conjugation of cDTPAa (Figs. 1e, S1). Besides, abundant functional groups such as hydroxyl (vO-H 3750–3300 cm−1) and carboxyl (vC=O 1630 cm−1, vO-H 2250–3500 cm−1) might originate from polymer-like coat of NCDDG could help maintain the water dispersity of NCDDG to enhance the proton hydration for more intensive MRI contrast ability and cover part of Gd3+ approach to the core. Another strong proof of Gd3+ from XPS survey spectrum where typical binding energies at characteristic peaks of C1s (283.6 eV and 285.8 eV), O1s (529.7 eV), N1s (397.7 eV), Gd 3d (1184.6 eV) and Gd 4d (141.4 eV) proved the existence of C (48.38%), N (6.51%), O (23.15%) and Gd (21.96%) in NCDDG further (Fig. 1f). Interestingly, the mass content of Gd3+ was basically consistent with the ICP-AES result (22.70%), which certified that Gd3+ was almost all modified on the surface of NCDD and thus led to the increased T1-weighted MRI contrast effect. In addition, zeta potential showing an upward trend (Fig. 1g) also confirmed the successful synthesis of NCDD and NCDDG due to the decrease of amino group and chelation of Gd3+.

In addition to some special properties mentioned above, NCDDG performing good fluorescence and enhanced MRI contrast ability in vitro. As shown in Fig. S2, the fluorescent spectra portrayed NCDDG, with a wide range of excitation and emission wavelengths, also exhibited excitation-dependent emission behavior similar to NCD. Cooperated with 2D fluorescence spectrum of NCDDG, the best excitation and emission wavelength was 370 nm and 463 nm, respectively (Figs. 2 a, S2 and S3). Therefore, the inserted optical image using a Canon camera showed the bright blue fluorescence of NCDDG solution under a UV lamp still because of the precursor (NCD). Overall, the above results indicated that the optical properties of NCDDG can be well-preserved though the attachment of cDTPAa and Gd3+ to NCD. Additionally, the fluorescence lifetime of NCDDG (7.42 ± 0.04 ns, X2 = 1.5) was higher than that of NCD (5.88 ± 0.011 ns, X2 = 1.38) through frequency-domain lifetime method where the λex was 370 nm and the λem was 450 nm (Fig. S4). The intriguing phenomenon that the fluorescence lifetime was prolonged might bear on the marriage of Gd3+ and DTPA which increased the packing and coiling of the polymer coated over the NCD (Hashim et al., 2014). Concerning the MR property, NCDDG possessed stronger T1 contrast ability than the commercial Gd-DTPA, which was proved by Fig. 2b and c. Above all, the T1W-MR image signal amplified with brighter images when the concentration of Gd3+ changed from 0 to 1.00 mM for both NCDDG and commercial Gd-DTPA. Comparatively, more obvious change occurred in NCDDG group (Fig. 2b). Then taking 1/T1 as the ordinate and Gd3 + concentration as the abscissa, the slope value of the curve represents r1 value. It was found that the r1 value of NCDDG (8.06 mM−1/s) was about 2.2 times that of the commercial Gd-DTPA (3.70 mM−1/s), indicating NCDDG’s potential to be a better T1 contrast agent (Fig. 2c). As reported, T1 or T2 contrast enhancement of MRI all matters the interaction of surrounding water protons and metal ions, which can shorten the longitudinal (T1) or transverse (T2) relaxation time of local water protons, increase longitudinal relaxation rate (r1) or transverse relaxation rate (r2) and thus present brighter T1-weighted MR images (higher signal intensity) or darker T2-weighted MR images (lower signal intensity) (Ni et al., 2017). Accordingly, here comes an assumption that NCDDG with a better T1W-MRI contrast effect probably is attributed to the more opportunity to contact with water molecules for the strengthened proton hydration of NCDDG highly coated with hydrophilic polymer. Besides, this small probe could be uptaken by human-derived U87 cells with few obstacles compared with the control (Fig. S5). We found that the uptake of NCDDG within U87 cells was concentration- and time-dependent. Among them, the best incubation concentration and time were 300 μg/mL and 2 h (Fig. 3 a and b). In addition, the favorable cell state of all the graphs showed that the biocompatibility of NCDDG was better than Gd-DTPA might due to the good shelter of the polymer which prevented Gd3+ chelated close to the core from leaking out of the system. This was also verified in Fig. 4 where the cytotoxicity of NCDDG to U87 cells was still lower than Gd-DTPA at 256 μg/mL of Gd3+ concentration.

Successful animal experiments were carried out for the imaging of NCDDG in vivo demonstrated again the potential of NCDDG to be an ideal dual-modal imaging nanoprobe for clinical application. As presented in Fig. 5 a, the margin and central part of tumor tissue showed similar T1 value changing trend but different degree over time. Obviously, the increased T1W-MR signal of margin to background exhibited more significant change than the center within the glioma (Fig. 5b). It suggested that though NCDDG could permeate the leaky microvascular walls and transport into the tumor site due to the harshness of NCDDG to accumulate in the rapidly-flowing blood circulation system, the penetration of NCDDG into the deeper tumor tissue was partly limited by the aggregation of NCDDG. It has been reported that, apart from physiological factors of the tumor, diffusivity and uptake rate of nanoparticles quite affect their penetration into the tumor as well (Dewhirst and Secomb, 2017). Therefore, it was a little tougher for larger nanoparticles to penetrate into the glioma and thus caused darker signal of central part and brighter signal of margin within the glioma over time in halfway but almost the same T1 value finally. Nevertheless, this unique ‘margin effect’ of our material is also promising to be applied in complete resection of glioma which often has an unclear definition of margin. As shown from the in vivo fluorescence images of glioma-bearing mice (Fig. 5c), several photos were taken at different time intervals to verify that NCDDG could be distributed over the body of mice including the tumor tissue compared with the control group injected with saline (Fig. S6a). Meanwhile, the accumulation of NCDDG in the brain was confirmed by stronger fluorescent signal compared with the control and the signal reached the maximum 1 h post-injection in accordance with the MRI result. After the completion of shooting fluorescent photos, mice were sacrificed and main organs including brain, heart, liver, spleen, lung and kidney were removed to obtain fluorescence images as shown in Figs. 5d and S5b. Fluorescence in liver and kidney was the strongest because the uptake of reticuloendothelial system (RES) was still the main in vivo process of NCDDG. In addition, Fig. 5e originated from a complete brain excised into two parts and the brightest position was proved to be the glioma, confirming there was relatively high uptake of glioma to NCDDG for the stronger fluorescence of tumor tissue in the excised brain than the control (Fig. S6c), further indicating that NCDDG could indeed target to tumor tissue via EPR effect.

Actually, MRI is reported to have high spatial resolution by offering more information such as the size, shape and position of glioma which has been proved by extensive preclinical researches and current clinical application effects of patients (Sosnovik and Weissleder, 2005; Na et al., 2009). Therefore, only fluorescent imaging realized by carbon nanodots can’t achieve this point mentioned above and this is why we designed to marry Gadolinium (III) ions to carbon nanodots. Furthermore, NCDDG as a NO donor might be decomposed into NO by nitric oxide synthase (NOS). Then the released NO took part in the activation of guanylate cyclase (cGMPase) which could catalase the synthesis of cGMP to enhance the permeability of the BBB and strengthen the EPR effect synergistically (Mayhan, 1999; Winter et al., 2008). Therefore, the glioma uptook more NCDDG and displayed stronger signal intensity both in T1-MR images and fluorescence images. Currently, though MRI and fluorescence imaging have their own unique advantages, the former one has obtained more clinical acceptance for its better resolution and precision than the latter one which is still promising to be further promoted (Pysz et al., 2010). Besides, in our opinion, we would suggest to adopt both of them because MRI is more suitable for preoperative or postoperative examination and fluorescence imaging has been studied a lot in the guidance of surgery to many kinds of tumors.

A dual-modal imaging system, where clinical Gd-DTPA was covalently married to the hydrophilic polymer-coated carbon nanodots, was facilely constructed. This system can not only reduce the toxicity and enhance the contrast ability of traditional Gd-DTPA due to the highly hydrophilic polymer coating and the consequently enhanced proton hydration, but also can increase the spatial resolution of carbon nanodots-based fluorescence imaging via MRI contrast. Attributed to the small particle size and hydrophilic polymer coating, this system exhibited prolonged in vivo circulation and enhanced retention effect, which therefore can overcome the BBB and permeate leaky microvascular walls into surrounding glioma tissues. As a result, sensitive dual-modal glioma-targeted MR/fluorescence imaging was synergistically achieved with high sensitivity and resolution, which would be a potential alternation for clinical glioma imaging contrast.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (81573002 and 81773280) and Shanghai Pujiang Program (17PJD002).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijpharm.2018.09.010.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
